Son of a Botanist Productions LLC — Confidential
"A young woman digs into her boyfriend's murder at an elite tennis club — and discovers her family has been running an Aztec death cult beneath it for a century, grooming her to be its next queen."
Supernatural Horror
Psychological Thriller
Social Horror
Story by
Marc Sternberg
Screenplay
Saul Espinoza & Marc Sternberg
Production
Seattle, WA — SAG-AFTRA MLB
Registered
WGAE © 2024
Script page count
98 pages
Estimated runtime
90 minutes
The film
Three things — inseparable
I
Class Warfare
The violence of legacy. What wealthy institutions do to talented poor children who dare to be better. The scholarship program as a trap. The tennis court as a rigged game.
II
Inherited Evil
The horror of discovering that the people who raised you built the world around you to consume you. That a mother can genuinely love her daughter and still intend to use her as a vessel.
III
The Refusal
Whether blood destiny can be refused. What it costs to break a cycle. Whether breaking it is even possible — or whether refusal is just the cycle's next phase.
The dungeon under the tennis courts is not a metaphor. It is where the metaphor goes to eat.
The world
A town divided by architecture
The surface
The Sneer Racquet Club
Founded 1925. The club presents itself as a bridge between working-class talent and legacy privilege. It is the opposite. Its architecture enforces separation. The scholarship program identifies talented children, brings them into proximity with legacy members — and when they inevitably excel, they are marked for sacrifice.

The club's logo is a single S. Every initiated cult member knows S stands for Salazar. Everyone else believes it stands for Sneer.
The underground
Huitzilopochtli's Chamber
Directly beneath the center courts. Accessed via the club's back office — when Kracell emerges through that staircase door in the climax, she understands simultaneously: she has been standing beneath the courts the entire film. The plaque at the cave entrance reads ESTABLISHED 1925 — ORDER MUST BE MAINTAINED. Below that, in the oldest stone: SALAZAR.

The 50-year succession cycle closes in 2025. The clock has run out.
Story summary
Three acts. One revelation.
Act One — The Beautiful Lie
Pages 1–38
Kracell has been quietly tracking the disappearances of scholarship children for months before her boyfriend Bobby is taken. The world looks like an elite tennis club. Its scholarship program looks like philanthropy. Seven missing children of color say otherwise. Tatum is the one cop who believes her. Bobby is abducted the night he finds the evidence. The investigation turns personal.
"The horror in Act One is entirely in the gap between what the world looks like and what it is."
Act Two — The Crack in the Lie
Pages 38–96
Tatum is killed. Kracell and Tee have no system left to appeal to. John Doe — the wrongly blamed suspect — becomes their only ally. The club's mythology begins surfacing: the blood ceremony she barely remembers, the jade bracelet she has worn since childhood, the carvings beneath the courts. The midpoint: the NYE blood binding ceremony replayed. Kracell in its center. She was never investigating from the outside.
"After Tatum, there is no system left to appeal to."
Act Three — The Choice
Pages 96–128
The dungeon. Joseph rescued. Bobby's evidence box recovered. Jack unmasked — her own father. The cellar: eight Polaroids, decades of children. Kracell breaks the necklace from Dotty's throat — and does not put it on. The fire. The refusal. The first fully self-determined act of her life. She emerges from below the courts into daylight. The bracelet is still on her wrist. The necklace survived the fire. Mary watched everything.
"This is where Kracell stops being the investigator and becomes the refuser."
Core plot points
Eight turns that build the trap
1
The dungeon cold open
Doe pinned to stone. His sister Mia killed by a steel-toed boot beside a Sneer Racquet Club scholarship certificate. Tatum in the evidence room, unable to stop opening the Polaroid box. The film begins mid-atrocity, before the audience knows what any of it means.
2
Bobby is taken
Bobby calls Kracell the night he finds the evidence. "Get the box in my closet. I wrote everything—" The line goes muffled. A Polaroid click in the dark. Silence. The investigation stops being about seven missing children and starts being about him.
3
Tatum is killed
The film's institutional ally — the one cop inside the system on their side — is removed. After Tatum, Kracell and Tee are on their own. The pivot point: the investigation must now operate outside any official structure entirely.
4
The NYE ceremony — replayed
The midpoint revelation. The blood binding ceremony Kracell barely remembered happened on New Year's Eve — Dotty cut her, collected her blood in a bowl, and delivered it to the beast below. Kracell was not witnessing a ritual. She was the ritual. She was never investigating from the outside.
5
The geography revealed
Kracell emerges from dungeon stairs into the club's back office. She looks up through a gap in the ceiling: the underside of Court 6's blacktop. She has been standing beneath the tennis courts. The dungeon is not in the forest. It is here. It has always been here. Blood Court.
6
Bobby's box
Three folders. Nine names, plus Marcus Webb — the tenth. Sheriff Kearney: law enforcement protection and evidence suppression. A photograph: Mary between Ted and Sue. A jade bracelet on an eight-year-old's wrist. Bobby knew. He was protecting Kracell before they took him. The box is his last act of love.
7
Jack unmasked
Kracell pulls the mask from the Masked Man. Her father's face. The same hands that gave her tennis lessons. Fifteen years of the stone in her stomach that had no name — now named. Dotty through the dungeon door, unbothered: "Did you think I didn't see your car?" Tee on the floor. Everything retroactively explained.
8
The refusal
Kracell breaks the necklace from Dotty's throat. Sets it on the dungeon floor. Does not pick it up. Does not put it on. The fire. The beast is fed — Dotty's death completes the blood transfer regardless — but there is no queen. The cycle is disrupted. Not broken. Jade does not burn. The necklace survived.
Character arc — Kracell Sneer, 18
Investigator → Heir → Refuser
Who she is at the start
Methodical, observant, certain the world is more wrong than people are admitting. Her bedroom wall is an evidence board — seven missing scholarship children, three colors of yarn. She has been tracking this for months before Bobby is taken. She is not a passive protagonist. The investigation is already underway when the film begins.

The jade bracelet on her wrist has been attuning her to Huitzilopochtli's frequency since childhood. This explains the obsessive drive, the certainty about Doe before she has any logical reason to trust him, the cold precision in her violence that slightly exceeds the situation. She is not evil. She is oriented toward evil, using tools the evil gave her, fighting the thing that made her.
Who she is at the end
She began investigating a mystery from the outside. She ends having discovered she was never on the outside. The mystery was built around her. Raised by a mother who loved her genuinely and intended to use her as a vessel — simultaneously, without contradiction. Both were real.

The refusal — breaking the necklace from Dotty's throat and not putting it on — is the first fully self-determined act of her life. Everything before it was shaped by the bracelet, the blood ceremony, Dotty's engineering, the cult's design. This one moment is hers.

The open question the film leaves: Is the bracelet still running? She won the battle. The war is ongoing.
Act One
The Investigator
Tracking missing children. Building the wall. Certain she is on the outside of a system she can expose.
Midpoint
The Revelation
She was in the center of the NYE ceremony. The bracelet. The blood. She was never outside the system. She was always its center.
Late Act Two
The Inheritor
Bobby's box in her arms. Two bracelets on her wrists. She looks at both for the first time as what they are. She stands. She descends.
Act Three
The Refuser
Breaks the necklace. Does not put it on. "I expected much more from a Salazar." The first self-determined act of her life.
"I expected much more from a Salazar." — Kracell to Dotty, final confrontation
Why this matters
The horror is the system
The dungeon under the tennis courts is not a metaphor. It is where the metaphor goes to eat.
Blood Court is not a film that uses genre as decoration. The supernatural horror is the class horror made literal — a belief system that identifies talented poor children, elevates them just enough to confirm the threat they represent, and then removes them. The Sneer Racquet Club scholarship program is the feeding mechanism for an ancient god, disguised as philanthropy. The tennis court is a rigged game. The dungeon is beneath it.
Class warfare made flesh
The scholarship program identifies talented working-class children of color, brings them into proximity with legacy wealth, and when they inevitably excel — they are marked. The cult's theology: those who rise above their station must be corrected. This is not allegory. It is the plot.
Inherited evil — and the choice to refuse it
A mother who genuinely loves her daughter and intends to use her as a vessel — simultaneously, without contradiction. The horror is not that love was fake. The horror is that it was real and it still led here. Kracell's refusal asks: what does it cost to break a cycle that was built before you were born?
Female agency at the center
Every major decision in the film belongs to a woman. Kracell investigates, refuses, chooses. Dotty engineers, accepts, completes. Mary watches, calculates, waits. The sequel belongs to her. This is not a film about men deciding women's fates — it is a film about women fighting, and sometimes completing, the fates built for them.
World building
A town designed to enforce its own hierarchy
Eastside Heights
Working-class neighborhood. Modest homes. Chain-link courts and cracked concrete. Children practice against painted walls with rackets worn to the grip. The talent here is real, hungry, and has nowhere adequate to go. This is where the scholarship program hunts.
Riverside District
Sprawling estates. Luxury cars. Private courts and private coaches. Their children have been given everything except the need to be great. Their technique is competent. The hunger is absent. They are the protected class — the cult's theology says so, and their privilege proves it daily.
The Sneer Racquet Club
The bridge that keeps them apart. Center courts for legacy members only. Sight lines designed so legacy members can observe scholarship players — but not the reverse. The club's logo is a single S. Initiated cult members know it stands for Salazar. Everyone else believes it stands for Sneer.
The dungeon — beneath the courts
Huitzilopochtli's chamber, the ritual sacrifice room, and the cellar of bodies lie directly beneath the center courts. Accessed via the club's back office. Ancient stone carved with Aztec social hierarchy — but someone has replaced the priests with tennis trophies. The Salazar name appears in three locations: dungeon door, office floor, estate plaque. Buried. Persistent.
The cave system — the operational backdoor
A natural cave network predating the club by centuries connects the dungeon to a cliffside exit in the forest. Used to move victims and bodies without being seen from the club. The iron door reads: ESTABLISHED 1925 — ORDER MUST BE MAINTAINED. Below that, in the oldest stone: SALAZAR.
Founded
1925
Sneer Racquet Club / Salazar cult established on American soil
Active since
100 years
Of continuous sacrifice beneath a functioning tennis club
Setting
Late 2024–2025
The 50-year succession deadline has arrived. The clock has run out.
The mythology
Huitzilopochtli — the beast beneath
Origins — Spanish colonial Mexico
The Salazar family were conquistadors who came to Mexico not to destroy Aztec religion — but to weaponize it. They became entangled with the cult worship of Huitzilopochtli, the Aztec sun deity and god of war, and its theology: those who rise above their station must be punished. Those born to privilege must be protected. The gods demand order.

When the Salazars came to America in the early twentieth century, carrying social stigma as Mexicans in Anglo elite society, they anglicized their name to Sneer. Founded the club in 1925. The mythology traveled with them. The beast was already here, waiting beneath the land the cave system occupied.
The beast's nature
Huitzilopochtli is not a metaphor. It is a genuine living entity — ancient, consuming, real. The Salazars did not create it. They found it, consecrated it, and built their institution around its hunger. It communicates in Nahuatl. It speaks rarely. It cannot be killed by conventional means — slitting its throat only temporarily incapacitates it. Fire is its most significant threat. Whether fire can truly kill it remains unresolved at Film 1's end.

In Film 1, Huitzilopochtli is never fully shown. Only suggested — the wrong-colored light from below, the sub-bass resonance, Tee's altered state when he comes back through the door.
The 50-year succession cycle
1925
Club founded. Eleanor Salazar becomes the first American queen at 19. Necklace placed. Aging halves. The 50-year cycle begins.
1975
Eleanor sacrificed at 69, appearing 57. Dotty becomes queen at 25. Jack receives the jade bracelet as co-leader. Ted and Sue installed as visible parents.
2025
Dotty scheduled to be sacrificed. Kracell designated heir. Dotty accepts her death. Kracell refuses the necklace. The cycle is disrupted — not broken.
20??
Mary is 8 years old. Patient in a way that 8-year-olds are not patient. She is already calculating. The sequel belongs to her.
The ceremony requires three things simultaneously: the reigning queen's sacrifice by fire; a new queen of Salazar bloodline; and the jade necklace willingly accepted. Bloodline without the necklace is a locked door. The necklace without willing acceptance is a key that does not turn. Kracell refused the key.
Rules of the world
The mythology has internal logic. It holds.
The jade objects — what they do
Dotty's necklace
Slows the reigning queen's aging to half speed continuously. Never leaves her throat. The instant it is removed, she begins reverting to her true biological age. In 2025, that is 75. The necklace is also the succession activation object — but only when willingly accepted.
Jack's bracelet
Co-leader rank artifact. Slows his aging to half speed as reward for complicity. Jack is not and will never be the queen — that hierarchy is physically visible on both their wrists every day.
Kracell's bracelet
Attunement object. Passive, always running. Does not grant power or slow aging — it orients Kracell toward Huitzilopochtli's awareness and makes her receptive to the necklace. Think of it as a radio antenna already tuned to the right frequency. The necklace is the broadcast.
Mary's bracelet
Newer. Earlier in the attunement process. Less shaped. More shapeable. Dotty's contingency queen — placed on an 8-year-old as a backup. Mary was in the closet on New Year's Eve. She watched. She was not afraid.
Rules the story must honor
The necklace cannot be transferred before the ceremony
It never leaves Dotty's throat until the moment of sacrifice. Not removable, not transferable, not referenced as available. Any scene suggesting otherwise violates the core mythology.
Huitzilopochtli is never directly shown
Its presence is communicated through wrong-colored moving light from below, sub-bass sound design and Nahuatl frequency, and Tee's altered state when he comes back through the door. The creature the audience builds is always worse than anything practical.
The beast is fed — but has no queen
Dotty's death in the fire technically completes the blood transfer regardless of ceremony. The beast is fed. But the succession transfer did not complete because Kracell refused the necklace. Jade does not burn. It survived. This is the instability that drives Film 2.
Willing acceptance is non-negotiable
Bloodline alone is not enough. The necklace alone is not enough. The heir must place it willingly. This is why Kracell's refusal disrupts rather than destroys the cycle — the door exists, the key exists, but she refused to turn it.
Scholarship as theology
The selection criterion is specific: a poor child defeating a wealthy child at tennis is, in Salazar belief, a cosmic violation of natural order. Huitzilopochtli demands correction. The tournament bracket is a kill list.
The Polaroid documentation
Every sacrifice is documented with a Polaroid. Eight pinned in the lower chamber at eye level. Below them, older photographs — faded, the paper gone brown. More children. Decades earlier. 1931. 1947. 1958. The cellar holds them all.
Kearney's protection
Sheriff James Kearney provides law enforcement protection and evidence suppression. The institution requires an inside man in the legal system. The cult has always had one. Bobby found his name before they found Bobby.
Franchise & sequel potential
Film 1 ends in disruption — not resolution
What Film 1 resolves
✓The 2025 succession ceremony is disrupted. Kracell refused the necklace.
✓The dungeon is burning. Dotty and Jack are inside it.
✓Bobby's evidence box is in Doe's hands.
✓Joseph is rescued. Tee and Kracell have chosen each other.
What Film 1 does not resolve
✗The necklace survived the fire. Jade does not burn. It is on the dungeon floor.
✗Kracell's bracelet is still on her wrist. The attunement is still running.
✗The beast is fed — but has no queen. Without management, it does not die. It simply hunts.
✗Kearney is alive. The law enforcement cover still exists.
✗Mary watched everything. She is 8 years old. She is patient. She is already calculating.
"Kracell saved Mary from the cult. She did not save the world from Mary. The cycle is not broken. It is delayed."
— Story Bible v5
The sequel's horror
The girl who refused the crown is now the guardian of the girl who wants it. Mary is 8. She has a bracelet. She has the bloodline. She was in that closet on New Year's Eve and she was not afraid. She is exactly what Kracell was — except Kracell didn't know. Mary does.
The market
Proven genre appetite
Social horror with a built audience. The financial template is consistent: modest budget, strong hook, word of mouth.
Get Out (2017)
Social horror using entrapment within a respectable institution to literalize systemic evil as conspiracy. Blood Court uses the same architecture — for class.
Hereditary (2018)
Protagonist discovers she has been groomed her entire life for a supernatural destiny she cannot escape. The maternal horror and cult succession are Blood Court's direct structural ancestors.
Ready or Not (2019)
Old-money privilege is literally murderous. Thriller mechanics, class rage. Blood Court shares the tonal register and the revelation that the institution was always going to consume her.
Immaculate (2024)
A young woman discovers its rituals are designed around her specifically. Slow-burn dread, institutional complicity, a protagonist who was chosen before she arrived.

Blood Court is a contained, high-concept genre film with a strong central hook. The financial comp set demonstrates consistent market appetite at this price point and profile. Shooting under SAG-AFTRA Moderate Low Budget Agreement in Seattle.
Comprehensive comps
Story · Tone · Budget & Return
Three buckets. Each serves a different part of the investor conversation.
Story comps
What Blood Court is structurally doing
Get Out 2017
Institutional predation disguised as welcome. A protagonist groomed before they arrive. Blood Court uses the same architecture — for class, not race.
Hereditary 2018
Family cult legacy — a protagonist who cannot escape bloodline. The maternal horror and cult succession are Blood Court's direct structural ancestors.
Midsommar 2019
Outsider drawn into a sealed ritual world and revealed as the chosen one. The dread builds from belonging, not from threat.
Black Swan 2010
Young woman groomed toward a role she doesn't understand. Elite institution. Psychological unraveling. The protagonist's destruction is the institution's success.
Promising Young Woman 2020
Female protagonist. Dark secret beneath a respectable surface. A hard ending that refuses to flinch. The institution's corruption is the point, not the backdrop.
Tone comps
Distribution path and audience register
Saint Maud 2019
Slow dread, psychological interiority, ambiguous supernatural. Shudder acquisition path. Blood Court's bracelet attunement subplot lives in the same unresolved register.
Talk to Me 2022
Brutal, no-compromise ending. A24 distribution. Massive ROI — $4.5M to $90M on word of mouth alone. Proof that uncompromising horror reaches a mainstream audience.
Immaculate 2024
Neon distribution model. Institutional complicity, slow-burn dread, a female protagonist chosen before she arrived. Direct tonal and structural parallel.
The Menu 2022
Class horror inside a sealed institution with a dark comedy edge that crossed over to mainstream. The tonal model for how Blood Court's social horror can play to a non-genre audience without softening.
Budget & return comps
Floor case → ceiling case
The Witch 2015
10×
$4M → $40M worldwide
Sundance floor case. Proves the model: contained period horror, no stars, strong hook, built its own audience from the ground up.
Ready or Not 2019
9.5×
$6M → $57M worldwide
Class rage and dark comedy in a sealed institution. The tonal and structural comp closest to Blood Court. Word of mouth carried it.
Talk to Me 2022
20×
$4.5M → $90M worldwide
The single best ROI case in recent genre history. No-compromise ending. A24. Proves uncompromising horror finds its audience at massive scale.
Parasite 2019
23×
$11M → $258M worldwide · The crossover ceiling
Class warfare as the engine. The same social horror architecture Blood Court is built on, translated to mainstream crossover. This is the ceiling case for investors — not a genre outlier, but a proof that class horror travels.
Story comps establish what Blood Court is doing narratively. Tone comps establish the distribution conversation and audience register. Budget & return comps establish the investment floor (The Witch, $4M) and the crossover ceiling (Parasite, $258M) — with Talk to Me as the closest recent proof of concept for ROI at this budget tier.

Washington State · Production Incentive Program
Up to 35% cash back on qualified in-state spend
Administered by Washington Filmworks under the Motion Picture Competitiveness Program (MPCP). Blood Court is well-positioned to qualify for the enhanced incentive tier.
30%
Base incentive
Cash rebate on qualified Washington in-state expenditures for motion pictures
+5%
Enhanced — story
For productions centering historically underrepresented communities. Blood Court qualifies on story content alone.
+5%
Enhanced — rural
For productions shooting at least half their Washington days in a designated rural county. 30 of 39 WA counties qualify.
How the math works at our mid-budget ($500K)
Total budget
$500K
Full production budget — must be raised in full before principal photography begins
Qualifying spend
~$250K
~50% of total budget estimated to qualify as Washington in-state expenditure
Achievable base
~$200K
~80% of qualifying spend is realistically achievable after documentation and review
Estimated rebate
~$70K
35% applied to ~$200K achievable base. Returned approximately 6 months after principal photography concludes.
What this is — and isn't
This is a cash rebate, not a tax credit and not upfront financing. The full production budget must be raised and spent before any rebate is issued. Washington Filmworks disburses the cash payment approximately six months after principal photography ends, once the completion package is reviewed and approved. Investors should plan on full budget requirement with the rebate as a post-production return of capital — not a budget offset.
Why Blood Court qualifies
Blood Court's story — an Aztec-heritage family narrative centered on historically underrepresented communities — positions the production strongly for the enhanced incentive tier on story content alone. Routing shoot days to eligible rural counties (30 of 39 Washington counties qualify) provides access to the additional rural enhancement. Both enhancements are capped at 10% combined, bringing the maximum to 35%.
All figures are estimates based on current MPCP guidelines (revised June 2025) and internal budget modeling. Actual rebate is subject to Washington Filmworks approval, annual funding allocation, and final completion package review. Application must be submitted and approved prior to the start of principal photography.
The team
The people behind the film
Investors back people. Here is who is driving Blood Court from development through production.
Producer · Story Originator
Marc Sternberg
Story originator and producer on Blood Court. Conceived the project — an Aztec blood cult beneath an elite tennis club — and has driven it from initial concept through screenplay development, production planning, casting strategy, and budget structuring. Son of a Botanist Productions LLC. Based in Orange, CA.
Screenwriter
Saul Espinoza
Screenplay by Saul Espinoza, from a story by Marc Sternberg. Currently on Draft v1.2 — 98 pages. WGAE registered. Espinoza's fee is structured as 1% of total production budget, aligning writer incentive directly with production outcome. Work-for-hire agreement in place.
Primary Director
Tierra 'TT' Frost
First-time feature director with a strong visual sensibility and connection to the material. Seattle-based. Bringing a personal and grounded directorial voice to the supernatural horror register Blood Court requires — dread built through interiority and visual precision rather than spectacle.
Co-Director
Dawit
Co-director on the feature. Collaborative creative partnership with TT Frost across the full production. Seattle-based. Additional key department heads — DP, production designer, composer — to be confirmed upon financing.
Production entity: Son of a Botanist Productions LLC · 890 N Shaffer St. · Orange, CA 92867 · Marcsternberg13@gmail.com · (480) 567-4021
Distribution strategy
How this gets to market — and to investors
The teaser trailer is the first distribution tool — built to open doors with streamers, genre festivals, and co-production partners before the feature is made.
Phase 1 — Teaser
A professional teaser trailer is in production now, designed specifically for the investor and studio audience. Target: streamers with active horror slates (A24, Neon, Shudder, Netflix Horror, Amazon MGM), genre festival programmers, and co-production partners. The teaser is the proof-of-concept — it demonstrates the visual world, tone, and lead performance before a single feature dollar is committed.
Phase 2 — Festival & Sales
Target festival path: Sundance, SXSW, Tribeca, Fantasia, Fantastic Fest — genre festivals with strong acquisition histories. A sales agent will be engaged prior to principal photography to maximize pre-sale opportunities internationally. Foreign pre-sales in genre-strong territories (UK, Germany, South Korea, Australia) can be structured to reduce investor exposure prior to domestic deal.
Phase 3 — Domestic Distribution
Target distribution partners: A24, Neon (Immaculate model), Shudder (Saint Maud model), Amazon MGM, and Netflix Horror. The financial comp set — Talk to Me, Ready or Not, Hereditary — demonstrates that contained social horror at this budget consistently finds distribution at multiples of production cost. Theatrical + streaming rights will be structured to maximize investor return.
Revenue streams
Domestic theatrical · International theatrical · Domestic streaming (SVOD/AVOD) · International streaming · VOD / digital rental and purchase · Physical media · TV licensing · Festival prizes and awards · Ancillary and merchandise rights · Sequel rights (franchise-capable property)
Why genre travels
Horror is one of the most internationally consistent genres in film — the core emotional register (dread, shock, release) translates across cultures without the language dependency of comedy or drama. The social horror frame — class warfare, institutional evil, family legacy — gives Blood Court above-average crossover potential to non-genre audiences, as demonstrated by Parasite ($258M) and Get Out ($255M).
Risk factors
What investors should understand going in
Film investment carries real risk. We believe the project is well-structured to mitigate the most significant risks — but we want investors to understand them clearly before committing capital.
Production risk
Films overrun budgets and schedules. Weather, cast illness, location access, and unforeseen creative challenges can increase costs.
Mitigation: 10% contingency built into all three budget tiers. SAG-AFTRA MLB agreement with defined rate structures. 20 shoot days in a single city (Seattle) minimizes logistics complexity. Two-location shoot design.
Distribution risk
Not all completed films find distribution. The independent film market is competitive and a theatrical release is not guaranteed.
Mitigation: Teaser-first strategy de-risks distribution by opening conversations with buyers before production begins. Genre films at this budget have a consistent secondary market (VOD, streaming) even without theatrical. Sales agent to be engaged pre-production.
Market risk
Audience tastes shift. A genre that performs well today may be oversaturated by release. Streaming platform acquisition budgets fluctuate with broader industry conditions.
Mitigation: Blood Court's social horror engine — class warfare, inherited evil, female agency — is thematically durable rather than trend-dependent. The comp films span 2015–2024, demonstrating consistent market appetite across a decade of shifting conditions.
Incentive risk
The Washington Filmworks rebate (~$70K estimated) is not guaranteed. Annual funding is allocated competitively and approval requires a completed application prior to photography.
Mitigation: The rebate is presented in this deck as a post-production return of capital, not an upfront offset. The production is budgeted to stand on its own without it. The rebate improves investor economics but is not required for the project to proceed.
First-time director risk
TT Frost is a first-time feature director. First features carry inherent execution uncertainty that experienced directors do not.
Mitigation: Co-director Dawit provides collaborative coverage. The genre comp set includes several first-feature directors (Hereditary — Ari Aster, Midsommar — Ari Aster, Talk to Me — the Philippou brothers) whose debuts became defining works precisely because the material was matched to a fresh voice.
Recoupment risk
Film investment is illiquid. Capital is committed for the full production and post-production period before any return is possible. There is no guarantee of profit.
Mitigation: Investor capital recoupment is structured ahead of net profit participation. The modest budget ($250K–$700K) means the recoupment threshold is achievable through secondary market performance (VOD, streaming) even without a major theatrical deal.
The ask
What we're raising — and what you get
Blood Court is seeking financing for the feature film at the mid-budget tier. Below is the structure of the raise and what it means for investors.
The raise
Target raise
Mid-budget target, incl. 10% contingency
$500K
Low budget floor
Minimum viable feature with full story intact
$250K
High budget ceiling
SAG-AFTRA MLB ceiling with full cast package
$700K
What investors receive
Recoupment of invested capital from first revenues, ahead of profit participation
Net profit participation proportional to investment share
Washington Filmworks rebate (~$70K estimated at $500K budget) returned to the production approximately 6 months post-photography — reduces effective cost basis
Executive producer credit available at qualifying investment levels
Participation in a franchise-capable property with sequel architecture already in place
Use of funds
Above-the-line talent, below-the-line crew and production, post production, general & administrative, and 10% contingency. Full budget breakdown available on request.
Revenue paths
Theatrical distribution, domestic and international streaming, VOD, festival sales, foreign pre-sales, and ancillary rights. Genre audience loyalty drives consistent secondary market performance.
Timeline
Teaser in production now. Feature principal photography targeted September 2026, financing dependent. SAG-AFTRA Moderate Low Budget Agreement. 20 shoot days. Seattle / PNW.
Investment Risk Disclosure
This presentation is for informational purposes only and does not constitute an offer to sell or a solicitation of an offer to buy any securities. Investment in motion pictures involves a high degree of risk. Past performance of any film, filmmaker, or production company is not indicative of future results. The motion picture industry is highly speculative. Most films do not recoup their production and marketing costs through theatrical release alone. Prospective investors should be aware that they may lose their entire investment. No guarantees are made regarding distribution, release, revenue, or return on investment. This presentation may contain forward-looking statements based on current expectations and assumptions. Actual results may differ materially due to factors including but not limited to: market conditions, distribution outcomes, competition, regulatory changes, and production contingencies. Prospective investors should consult with their own legal, tax, and financial advisors before making any investment decision. Investment in Son of a Botanist Productions LLC and/or Blood Court is suitable only for accredited investors who can bear the loss of their entire investment. This offering has not been reviewed or approved by the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission or any state securities authority.
